segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Listen to a talk in a biology class.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: In the early 1900s, sea otters, marine mammals that feed on invertebrates, including sea urchins, were nearly wiped out along the United States Pacific coast by fur hunters.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: With otters gone, urchin numbers exploded, destroying kelp forests.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Kelp is the type of seaweed that is sea urchins' favorite food.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: This left countless marine species without the protective habitat that kelp forests provided to them.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: In 1972, hunting sea otters was banned.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: As otters made a comeback, urchin numbers dropped, kelp forests grew back, and biodiversity flourished.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Scientists realized otters were a keystone species, organisms whose impact on ecosystems is far greater than their numbers suggest.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: A similar case occurred in Yellowstone National Park.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Wolves, the park's top predator, had been eradicated.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Populations of elk, a deer-like animal and a favorite prey for wolves, soared, and their huge populations damaged local vegetation, including the willow and aspen trees for which the park had been famous.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: In the 1990s, wolves were reintroduced to the park to get the elk population under control.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: But the wolves didn't just cut elk numbers; they changed elk behavior.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Afraid of being eaten by wolves, the elk avoided streamside areas where they're especially vulnerable.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: This helped willow and aspen trees reestablish themselves there, and the trees brought back beavers, animals that live in streams and build dams to create ponds for themselves.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: The beaver dams created habitats for fish, birds, and amphibians, a huge cascading effect because of this other keystone species.
